summary: A 2.4 million sq ft, 300 MW data center approved in June 2024 in Vineland's Urban Enterprise Zone without required neighbor notification. Residents discovered the project after construction began. Opposition focuses on threats to the Kirkwood-Cohansey aquifer, noise from 24/7 operations, and air pollution from 36 on-site natural gas generators producing up to 403 MW. City Council approved PILOT tax agreement despite resident opposition in January 2026.

Monroe Township Council adopted an amendment to the Hexa Builders Redevelopment Plan adding data centers as permitted use. Residents suspect a proposed 1.6 million sq ft warehouse is actually a data center in disguise. A variance for 1.4 million gallons/day of water was granted. The Pinelands Commission is reviewing the application and can approve or deny the changes, giving residents a key avenue to intervene.

A 795-acre data center proposed on farmland in south Joliet initially put on indefinite hold in 2025 after resident alarm, then reactivated. Plan Commission voted 7-1 to recommend approval after a 5-hour meeting where roughly 80 people spoke, mostly in opposition. Developers promise 7,000 construction jobs, 700 permanent jobs, and $310M in property taxes over 30 years. City Council vote scheduled for March 16, 2026.

Cloud Centers LLC withdrew its application for a 256,000 sq ft, 50 MW data center at the former Lockformer property at 711 Ogden Ave following mounting public opposition. Over 300 residents packed the planning commission hearing, exceeding capacity. The first hearing was canceled due to overcrowding. 127 residents submitted letters objecting. The site has environmental baggage from toxic chemical contamination of local drinking water.

Yorkville City Council approved Project Cardinal, a 1,037-acre data center campus with 14 buildings totaling 17 million sq ft, after a nearly 6-hour contentious meeting ending at 12:51 AM. Most residents who spoke opposed the project, calling for a moratorium and public referendum. Developer committed $51M in community payments over 4 years for schools, fire services, and nonprofits. A resident lawsuit against the city was also filed.

Constellation Energy purchased over 600 acres near Essex and is seeking rezoning and annexation. Much of the land was sold by village board members or their relatives. The Essex Coalition launched a petition urging industrial protections and is working to revive the village's dormant zoning board of appeals. Constellation has not officially announced a data center but residents fear one is planned given the site's proximity to power infrastructure.

About two dozen south and southwest suburban residents gathered at a Frankfort forum organized by environmental advocates to voice concerns about data center proposals in Joliet, Minooka, Essex, Coal City, and Yorkville. Residents fear higher utility bills, regional power outages, declining air quality, and strained water supplies from the cluster of proposed facilities in the greater Will County area.

Kosciusko County Area Plan Commission unanimously denied (8-0) Prologis's rezoning request for 554 acres of farmland near Leesburg from agricultural to industrial for a 10-building data center campus. County Commissioners upheld denial 3-0 on April 22. Over 100 residents attended, and a petition collected 1,000+ signatures. Residents cited loss of farmland and impact on the local Amish farming community.

Washington Township in northwestern Franklin County passed a 90-day moratorium on data centers in unincorporated parts of the township on December 8, 2025. The township also asked the city of Dublin, which the township encompasses, to adopt a moratorium as well. The action follows the broader wave of Central Ohio communities pushing back against the data center boom centered on New Albany.

South Bloomfield preemptively eliminated all areas of industrial zoning to prevent data center applications, after watching backlash to the industry in neighboring communities like Ashville. No data center developer had approached the village, but officials acted proactively to block the possibility entirely.

St. Albans Township held a public hearing on March 10, 2026 on a proposal to become the first township in Licking County to ban data centers. The township sits on the eastern edge of the New Albany data center corridor, home to 40+ data centers in western Licking County. Residents expressed concerns about water and electricity consumption from the nearby facilities.

Atmosphere Data Centers proposes multiple hyperscale data centers at the former Dickerson coal-fired power plant site, just outside Montgomery County's Agricultural Reserve. Opposition groups warn about Potomac River water consumption (which supplies 75% of regional drinking water), greenhouse gas emissions undermining county climate goals, and industrialization of a rural area. County Council voted unanimously Feb. 2, 2026 to pause data center permitting through end of 2026.

opposition_groups: Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee (DCRC)
summary: The Frederick County DCRC collected 15,000+ signatures to put a ballot question before voters opposing the County Council's creation of a 2,615-acre data center development zone near Adamstown. Frederick County had a 6-month moratorium in 2025 while debating regulations, but the council ultimately expanded the potential footprint. The referendum committee aims to let voters decide data center limits in the fall 2026 election.
The town of Shutesbury will consider its own data center moratorium during an annual town meeting in May 2026, following the precedent set by Lowell's 360-day moratorium enacted in March 2026.

Oppidan, an Excelsior-based developer, paused its data center project in Hampton after citing that the state would take too long to permit backup generators. Minnesota's permitting process for diesel backup generators takes 12-18 months. Amazon also abandoned its Becker data center after regulators required a major state permit for 250 diesel generators.

Microsoft canceled plans for a data center on 240+ acres in rural Caledonia after vocal community opposition. About 40 people spoke against the project during 2+ hours of public comment at a Plan Commission meeting. Despite the commission approving the rezoning, Microsoft stated it would not move forward 'based on the community feedback we heard.' The company redirected to its Mount Pleasant location instead.

Two Creeks, along with Two Rivers and Mishicot, held a joint town board meeting and approved identical resolutions calling on Manitowoc County to adopt a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction, with option to extend 6 months. These three northeast Wisconsin towns acted proactively to prevent data center siting in their communities.

Two Rivers joined Two Creeks and Mishicot in approving identical resolutions at a joint town board meeting calling for a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction in Manitowoc County, with option for 6-month extension.

Mishicot joined Two Creeks and Two Rivers in approving identical resolutions at a joint town board meeting calling for a one-year moratorium on data center permits and construction in Manitowoc County, with option for 6-month extension.

In Janesville, a proposal from Viridian to redevelop the retired GM plant into a data center campus is headed directly to voters in a November ballot question. Janesville was one of four Wisconsin communities that signed nondisclosure agreements shielding data center proposals from public view, fueling backlash.

Armada Township enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development on January 14, 2026, joining a growing wave of Michigan communities pausing data center permits while they develop local zoning rules.
Dundee Township enacted a 90-day data center moratorium which was subsequently extended. The township is among dozens of Michigan communities implementing temporary pauses on data center development.
Lodi Township in Washtenaw County enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development on February 3, 2026, joining the growing wave of Michigan townships pausing permits.

Manchester Township enacted a 2-year moratorium on data center development on October 14, 2025 -- one of the longest moratorium periods among Michigan communities. This is the most aggressive local response in the state.
Northville enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development on January 6, 2026, joining Springfield Township and other metro Detroit communities scrambling to regulate data centers.
Pittsfield Township in Washtenaw County enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development on November 18, 2025.
Grand Blanc Township enacted a 1-year moratorium on data center development, joining the wave of Michigan communities implementing temporary pauses while drafting zoning regulations.
South Lyon enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development on February 10, 2026. The city is adjacent to Lyon Township in Oakland County, where a data center has already been approved.
Saginaw enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development, joining the expanding list of Michigan communities implementing temporary pauses while developing local regulations.
Hayes Township enacted a 180-day moratorium on data center development.
Lenox Township enacted a 4-month moratorium on data center development with an extension option.
Sylvan Township in Washtenaw County enacted a 6-month moratorium on data center development.

Wildcat Ridge is the first of six data center campuses proposed in Archbald and one of at least 12 across Lackawanna County. At a 4-hour public hearing in March 2026, a raucous crowd drowned out testimony from developers. The project would consume up to 3.3 million gallons of water per day and require 1,600 MW of power -- more than the entire Lackawanna Energy Center can produce. If all Archbald proposals proceed, data centers could cover 14% of the borough.

A proposed 1,300 MW data center in Adams County would consume 31 times the county's total electricity consumption annually. Activist Nikki Gerber created a 1,300-member Facebook group 'Adams County for Responsible Development' to organize opposition. Power lines from three utilities (AES, Duke Energy, AEP Ohio) would be needed. AEP customers separately oppose a $400M rate hike driven in part by data center demand on the grid.

New Castle County Council is debating data center zoning regulations amid backlash to the Starwood Digital Ventures 'Project Washington' (a 1.2 GW, 580-acre proposal in Delaware City denied by DNREC as heavy industry incompatible with the Coastal Zone). Over 1,500 people voiced support for strong guardrails, but community advocates said the ordinance was 'watered down' to get necessary votes. Seven council members voiced opposition to the rules.

St. Charles, which in August 2025 became the first U.S. city to enact a citywide data center construction ban (one-year moratorium unanimously approved by all 10 council members after the secret 'Project Cumulus' caused public outcry), is now considering a permanent ban. The vote on the permanent ban was tabled until April 2026.

Lowell City Council voted to impose a 360-day moratorium on new data center construction and expansion, following years of noise complaints from neighbors of the 50 MW Markley Group facility (built on the former Prince Macaroni Manufacturing site). Councilor Kim Scott, representing the Sacred Heart neighborhood where the data center operates, initiated the motion to require updated zoning including buffer zones, noise standards, and utility rate protections.
